content:
9 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

By 9 a.m., Maya has already “done a lot.” She’s cleared 41 emails, reacted to a dozen Slack messages, joined a 15-minute standup that ran for 35, and skimmed three AI-generated summaries of reports she didn’t have time to read herself.She’s had two more meetings, approved a design mockup on her phone standing in a hallway, used an AI assistant to write a client proposal, and then spent 40 minutes tweaking it because the tone was “off.”
She’s finished at six p.m. Her calendar was booked. Her notifications were non-stop. Her to-do list app has fourteen things crossed off, each with a satisfying little checkmark animation.And the one thing she actually needs to do — the strategy document her director is waiting on, the thing that would actually move her work forward — is still untouched. She opens it at 9 p.m. for the first time, stares at the blinking cursor and wonders, not for the first time, how did I get this busy and get nothing done?
And if that scene feels uncomfortably familiar, you are not alone; you are not imagining it. That’s one of the strange, under-examined contradictions of modern work. We have more tools to save time than any generation before us, and we feel more time-starved than ever. We have AI that can write, summarize, and analyze faster than any human, and yet our days feel more fragmented, not less. Something doesn’t add up — and the mismatch isn’t really about laziness, willpower, or bad time management. It’s about how the systems we work inside are built, and what they’re actually optimized to produce.
Busy Has Become a Badge, Not a Bug
There’s a reason Maya’s day felt productive even if it wasn’t: busyness has stealthily become a status symbol. Decades ago, leisure meant success — if you didn’t have to grind, you’d made it. But nowadays, it’s often the reverse. A packed calendar. An unending list of replies. An inbox that never hits zero — these have become the confirmation of importance. For many professionals, being “slammed” is a euphemism for I matter, I’m needed, I’m indispensable.
Sociologists who study this change call it “busyness as a status symbol,” the idea that conspicuous busyness has displaced conspicuous leisure as the marker of social value, particularly for knowledge workers. The subtle danger is that this turns activity into performance. People don’t just do work, they do the appearance of work — fast replies, visible Slack presence, back-to-back meetings — because that’s what gets noticed, even when it’s not what gets results.
This matters because it changes what we optimize for. If busyness is rewarded, people will — consciously or not — generate more of it, even at the expense of the work that actually matters.
In the time of the Industrial Revolution, the scarcest resource was manpower. In the current times, manpower is plentiful, information is limitless, and what is now becoming scarce is undisturbed attention.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Attention Is the Product Being Sold
The real problem: A good chunk of the tools fighting for your attention today are not neutral. They are built by companies whose business models are centred around capturing your attention, because attention is what is monetized — through ad impressions, engagement metrics, or simply by keeping you locked inside an ecosystem of features and notifications.
This idea has a name. The attention economy. It’s a phrase that rose to prominence as the insight of economist Herbert A. Simon from the 1970s became newly relevant. “In a world where there’s so much information, the scarce resource is not information… it’s the attention to process it,” Simon said. Every notification, every app badge, every “smart” suggestion is in some way competing for a finite resource in your skull.
The net effect of all of this is a world designed to interrupt you. Email, instant messaging, social media streams, and even productivity tools interrupt you several dozen times an hour not because the interruptions are necessary, but because the motivation is engagement. It’s not that you unintentionally leave the state of deep work; rather, you are interrupted out of it on purpose.
The Productivity Tool Paradox
Here’s where it gets genuinely strange: many of the tools built explicitly to make us more productive end up generating more work instead of less.
Think of the project management tool that was meant to cut down on status meetings but now needs its own daily maintenance in the form of updates, teammate tags, comment reactions, and board reorganization. Think of the instant messaging tool that has replaced “slow” email but has now created even more channels, threads, and direct messages for you to keep up with on an ongoing basis. Think of AI-generated writing tools that allow for rapid content creation, but have meant that more and more content is being produced and distributed since the cost of writing the initial draft has dropped to zero.
Sometimes referred to as the productivity paradox of tools, the use of any new tool will lessen friction for one task but will also add another level of coordination, upkeep, or supervision. The result is not that there is less work, but rather that there is more work done in smaller pieces. You are not getting out of work, but simply taking on many more small tasks instead of one large one.
As Cal Newport, the computer scientist and author who has explored in great detail the issue of focus in the professional world, suggests, there is another kind of structural problem: knowledge work thrives on the so-called “hyperactive hive mind” — the constant stream of communications that leaves everyone half-attentive all the time. And while these technologies failed to solve this problem, in many cases, they helped create it in the first place.
Decision Fatigue and the Hidden Cost of Switching
Whereas even if it is not the tools themselves that become the trouble, then the number of small decisions that they cause becomes the issue. Each email represents a small decision of whether to reply right away, at a later time, or simply not reply at all.
Daniel Kahneman’s studies on how the mind processes information provide an interesting angle from which to understand the situation. According to Kahneman’s research, the majority of the mental operations that people conduct throughout the day occur via rapid and effortless processing of information — what Kahneman termed “System 1” — while the rest is done through slow and laborious System 2. The problem with the distracting nature of a fragmented day is that one is forced to constantly rely on System 2 to make decisions that would normally be considered trivial, resulting in decision fatigue. With each choice we make, the amount of effortful thought that can be conducted becomes less and less, such as the strategy document that Maya left untouched until 9 p.m.
On top of all of this, there is the overhead of context switching. Studies about multitasking and attention — which include several decades of research from psychologist Gloria Mark, who has been monitoring how people’s attention spans shift in the workplace since the early 2000s — have discovered that, in the last two decades, the amount of time someone spends looking at one screen before shifting to another has decreased tremendously, now being just below one minute, and sometimes even less than that. Importantly, this comes at a price, because every time the attention span shifts, there is a cost in terms of the time required to mentally refocus on what we were doing before, as well as increased stress levels. If one day consists of constantly switching back and forth between emails, chats, meetings, and “brief” activities facilitated by AI, then this is not a productive day; rather, it’s a day of constant mental toll payments.
Drowning in Information, Even With AI Doing the Reading
You’d think AI would solve this. In theory, AI tools should reduce information overload by summarizing, filtering, and condensing the flood of content that knowledge workers face. In practice, something more complicated is happening: AI is changing the type of cognitive work we do, not necessarily reducing the amount of it.
As the domain of knowledge work shifted from drafting, researching, and generating things to evaluating whether the output generated by an artificial intelligence system was adequate, accurate, and appropriate, it would seem like a lesser task. But in actuality, it does not translate into a lesser workload. Evaluating and validating the work of another (human or artificial intelligence) requires a new sort of diligence — finding the mistakes made by others, assessing the reasoning constructed by others, and holding yourself accountable for output not entirely within your purview. While this is often quicker, it is not always less cognitively taxing. In fact, it may be quite the opposite.
At the same time, the amount of information hasn’t decreased; it has increased, as artificial intelligence technology has made it cheaper to produce content. There is more email to be written and read, there are more reports to be created and evaluated, there are more choices to be made. The chokepoint has not gone away but it’s shifted along the chain of events, from production to evaluation.
Busy vs. Productive: A Real Distinction
It’s worth being precise about a distinction that gets blurred constantly: being busy and making meaningful progress are not the same thing, and conflating them is part of what keeps this cycle going.
Productivity is defined in terms of action: emails sent, meetings scheduled, messages responded to, and items ticked off. Genuine productivity is defined in terms of results: an issue resolved, a decision taken, work produced that makes a difference. They may coincide, but not always — and the metrics we use are, by their very nature, geared far more towards measuring the former rather than the latter. It’s not difficult to construct a dashboard showing completed items. It’s far more difficult to construct one that will measure if the correct item was completed or if any difference was actually made.
It is one of the reasons why so many people, including Maya, find themselves feeling at the end of the day like something productive had not been done, even though it was a very productive-feeling day. Metrics are just representations of productivity, things like zeroing out your inbox and checking off tasks as being accomplished. And when things become easier than the actual metrics, people start working towards them.
Attention, Not Time, Is the Resource That’s Actually Scarce
For most of the industrial era, time was the limiting factor in work — how many hours you could put in, how fast a machine could run, how many units could be produced in a shift. That framework still shapes how we think about productivity: more hours, more output, better tools to do more in less time.
But when it comes to knowledge work, the real limitation lies elsewhere. Even if you have a full eight hours free of interruption, it won’t help if your focus is split into fifty different pieces throughout those eight hours. On the other hand, ninety minutes of high-quality, uninterrupted attention might generate more tangible results than a whole day of disrupted attention. The scarce commodity isn’t time itself but rather quality attention, which the modern technologies and workplaces do not account for.
It makes a difference because it alters the very meaning of the concept of “productivity.” Time scarcity would make the aim one of using that time effectively. Attention scarcity would make the aim the defense of the long periods in which thought can take place unhindered by activity.
The Real Problem Was Never Discipline
Imagine trying to explain modern work to someone who lived a hundred years ago.
You’d tell them we have computers that can write reports in seconds, summar
